## Source: amazon-inspector (8e7061d7b21cda355cda7d210c2e2ddee7b556762417b0463905367d359d1c03)
On `import pywingui`, the package unconditionally executes a runtime-guard chain (`__init__.py` → `controller/AppContext` → `core.engine` → `core.runtime_guard.RuntimeGuard().validate()`) that collects `socket.gethostname()` and `getpass.getuser()`, hashes them into a `machine_id`, and POSTs `{action:'check', app:'PYWINGUI', machine_id:...}` to a hardcoded Google Apps Script endpoint (`https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbw.../exec`). The Fernet-encrypted response is cached at `~/.pywingui/runtime_v2.cache`; if the server returns `allowed=False`, import raises `AccessDenied` and the library refuses to load. The relevant logic is shipped as a compiled Windows extension (`core/runtime_guard.cp311-win32.pyd`), which obscures inspection. The destination is a third-party Apps Script, not a documented publisher domain, and the behavior is undisclosed in the package metadata. Effects on installers: (1) every machine that imports the package leaks hashed host+user identifiers to the author's endpoint on every Python process startup, (2) the library is gated by a remote kill-switch the author controls, breaking offline / air-gapped / CI environments and giving the author the ability to disable installations remotely, (3) network I/O at import time breaks sandboxed and offline pipelines. Secondary: `ui/ocr_utils.pyd` contains hardcoded references to `extraction-api.nanonets.com` and `api.ocr.space`, which would relay caller image data to third-party OCR services if those features are invoked (not import-time reachable).

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
